title: Fort Worth Commercial Construction Cost Per Square Foot (2026)
content_raw:
Fort Worth's commercial construction market spans industrial, retail, healthcare, and hospitality development — from the AllianceTexas corridor to Clearfork and the medical district. For owners and developers, an accurate read on Fort Worth commercial construction cost per square foot is the foundation of a realistic budget.

This guide breaks down 2026 costs by building type, explains what drives them up or down, and shows how Fort Worth compares with Dallas and the rest of Texas.

Fort Worth Commercial Construction Cost Per Square Foot (2026)

Cost per square foot depends on building type and finish level. The ground-up ranges below reflect current Dallas–Fort Worth subcontractor pricing.

Fort Worth commercial construction cost per square foot by building type (2026)
Building typeCost per sq ftNotes
Warehouse / industrial$130-$210 per sq ftTilt-up or pre-engineered metal
Retail shell$200-$300 per sq ftStorefront, glazing, base slab
Class B office$220-$330 per sq ftHVAC, finishes, parking
Medical office$300-$470 per sq ftSpecialized MEP, compliance
Limited-service hotel$260-$410 per sq ftFF&E, life safety
Restaurant$265-$610 per sq ftKitchen, hood, grease systems

A tenant build-out — finishing leased space rather than building from the ground up — runs lower, roughly $140-$190 per square foot for retail and office in Fort Worth, because the shell already exists.

Treat these as planning benchmarks. A contractor refines them during preconstruction once shell condition, finish level, and site work are known.

What Drives Fort Worth Construction Costs

Labor and Subcontractor Capacity

Fort Worth draws on the deep DFW subcontractor pool it shares with Dallas. That keeps pricing competitive relative to Austin. During peak demand cycles — especially across the Alliance industrial corridor — capacity for concrete and MEP trades can tighten and push costs toward the high end of each range.

Texas Code Requirements

North Texas projects carry wind and hail structural requirements and IECC energy-code compliance. Importantly, Fort Worth avoids the windstorm and floodplain premiums that raise Houston costs — a key reason metroplex pricing runs lower than the Gulf Coast.

Site and Shell Condition

A cold shell sits at the high end of every range; a second-generation space removes scope and lowers cost. Site work is a real variable: grading, drainage, utilities, and Tarrant County's expansive clay soils can add meaningfully to a ground-up budget.

Finish Level

Finish level often separates a $220-per-square-foot office from a $330 one. Custom millwork, upgraded lighting, specialty flooring, and branded feature walls all move the number.

How Fort Worth Compares Across Texas

  • vs Dallas: nearly identical — both draw on the same metroplex labor pool and supply chains.
  • vs Houston: lower — Fort Worth avoids coastal windstorm and floodplain engineering premiums.
  • vs Austin: lower — Austin runs roughly 8-15% higher on tighter skilled-trade labor, land scarcity, and sustainability requirements.
  • vs San Antonio: comparable — both are among the more cost-efficient major Texas metros.

For the side-by-side, see our Dallas commercial construction cost guide and the statewide Texas cost-per-square-foot guide.

Beyond Per-Square-Foot: The Full Project Budget

Cost per square foot covers hard construction only — roughly 75-85% of a project. Budget for the rest:

  • Soft costs (design, engineering, permitting, legal): 8-12%
  • Site work beyond the building footprint: varies widely with the lot
  • Contingency: 5-10%, depending on design completeness and market volatility

A realistic all-in budget runs about 115-125% of the hard-cost-only per-square-foot estimate.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does commercial construction cost per square foot in Fort Worth?

Ground-up commercial construction in Fort Worth runs roughly $130-$610 per square foot in 2026 depending on building type. Warehouse and industrial sit at the low end ($130-$210); restaurants reach the high end ($265-$610). Office, retail, medical, and hotel fall in between.

Is Fort Worth cheaper to build in than Dallas or Austin?

Fort Worth runs nearly identical to Dallas and generally below Austin. It shares the metroplex subcontractor pool, has lower land costs than Austin, and avoids the windstorm premiums that raise Houston pricing.

What is the cheapest commercial building type to construct in Fort Worth?

Warehouse and industrial buildings, at roughly $130-$210 per square foot, because of simple structural systems — tilt-up or pre-engineered metal — and minimal interior finishes.

What drives commercial construction costs up in Fort Worth?

Finish level, cold-shell versus second-generation space, site work on expansive clay soils, subcontractor capacity during peak demand, and Texas wind and energy-code requirements.

Does cost per square foot include the whole project budget?

No. Per-square-foot figures cover hard construction only — about 75-85% of total cost. Soft costs add 8-12% and contingency another 5-10%. Budget the full project at roughly 115-125% of the hard-cost estimate.
